meta.review_field: global health (health economics / epidemiology of childhood disability)
meta.review_note: Deterministic verdict preserved verbatim (DESIGN §3.1): the single percent_change.v1 FAIL (c1, severity B, deterministic_confirmed, reported -9% vs recomputed -12%, with reproducing recompute_script) is kept byte-for-byte and was NOT overridden; no flag manufactured. The load-bearing number was recomputed before any advisory (HONESTY): 1 - aRR(0.88) = 12.0%, matching the corrigendum (PMC11237678) and confirming the reported 9% is wrong (9% would require aRR=0.91). The paper's lone claim is already adjudicated by the flag, so the abstained list is empty; the panel adds advisories code cannot settle (skeptic: the 'approximately' hedge does not rescue a 3pp gap; domain-expert + methodologist: 1-aRR conversion and 'exact' strength miscalibration; integrity-screener: error is conservative/benign and already corrected) plus one irreducibly subjective dimension (materiality of 9% vs 12% to the conclusion, surfaced not scored, DESIGN §3.5).

findings.message: reported percent change does not match the recomputed old->new change

evidence.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)

location.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)

routed_to_human.note: SKEPTIC. The deterministic percent_change.v1 FAIL stands and is not softened by the 'approximately' hedge: 1 - aRR(0.88) = 12.0% (recomputed), and 12% is not 'approximately 9%' -- the gap is 3 percentage points, i.e. the true reduction is ~33% larger in relative terms than reported. No reading of aRR=0.88 yields 9% (that would need aRR=0.91). This advisory does not override or duplicate the flag; it records that the only available hedge fails to rescue the number.
routed_to_human.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)

routed_to_human.note: DOMAIN-EXPERT (health economics / epidemiology). An adjusted rate/risk ratio of 0.88 expresses a 12% relative reduction (1 - aRR), the standard epidemiological reading; the paper's '9% fewer' misapplies that conversion. Strength 'exact' is miscalibrated for a value the authors themselves formally corrected (corrigendum PMC11237678 -> 12%). The substantive direction (disability associated with FEWER play opportunities) is faithful; only the magnitude conversion is wrong. Code settles the arithmetic (the flag); the strength/scope miscalibration is routed.
routed_to_human.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)

routed_to_human.note: METHODOLOGIST (T3, method appropriateness). Reporting a relative reduction as 1 - aRR against an implicit reference of aRR=1.0 (no difference) is the correct method; the error is purely in executing that subtraction (0.88 -> reported 9% instead of 12%), already captured deterministically. Beyond the arithmetic there is no test-choice or assumption defect to adjudicate here, and a single reported aRR gives code nothing further to recompute. Routed as method-framing context, not a second flag (DESIGN §3.1: only a calibrated verifier renders a verdict).
routed_to_human.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)

routed_to_human.note: INTEGRITY-SCREENER (T7). Low / no-action signal. The error direction is CONSERVATIVE -- it understates the disability play-gap (9% < true 12%), the opposite of result-inflating spin -- and is consistent with a benign transcription/rounding slip rather than manipulation. A published corrigendum (PMC11237678) already corrected the figure to 12%. No human escalation on integrity grounds is warranted; surfaced for completeness only.

SUBJECTIVE (T8, surfaced not scored -- DESIGN §3.5). Whether the 9%-vs-12% misstatement is material to the paper's substantive conclusion about unequal play opportunities for children with disabilities is an interpretive / significance judgement: both magnitudes support the same qualitative finding (a meaningful reduction), so a reader must decide how much the understated magnitude matters for the paper's claims and any policy reading. LITMUS confirms the arithmetic error (the flag) but does not score its importance.
routed_to_human.quote: children with disabilities had approximately 9% fewer play opportunities (aRR 0.88)
